articles.title: Scale P-CSCF registration workers during REGISTER surges
articles.summary: Use horizontal scale-out when REGISTER queues, retry storms, and high P-CSCF CPU all rise together.
content: When to use: REGISTER volume rises faster than the edge can absorb and the queue depth grows on the P-CSCF path.
Action: Add replicas on the registration-facing P-CSCF deployment, verify the service endpoints refresh, and spread traffic evenly before accepting more load.
Why it works: Scaling the first saturated SIP hop reduces request queuing and shortens the time window where UEs decide to retransmit.
Verify: REGISTER latency, retransmission count, and 401 or 202 backlog should drop within one refresh cycle.
Rollback: Scale down only after the registration curve stays flat and retry amplification is gone.

articles.title: Tame REGISTER retry amplification before it becomes a storm
articles.summary: Reduce the retry loop by checking timers, challenge pacing, and scenario-generator rate limits.
content: When to use: The retransmission counter climbs much faster than the unique subscriber count and the same Call-ID patterns repeat.
Action: Confirm whether the load is synthetic, lower the traffic generator rate if needed, and verify UE retry timers are not overly aggressive for the observed latency.
Why it works: Many registration incidents are sustained by retries rather than fresh subscribers, so damping the retry loop restores stability faster than scaling alone.
Verify: Duplicate REGISTER attempts per subscriber decline and 401 to 200 conversion improves.
Operator note: Capture a few repeating Call-ID samples so the RCA can tie the incident to retry amplification instead of organic growth.

articles.title: Throttle the synthetic registration burst at the traffic source
articles.summary: If the surge is caused by SIPp or another generator, slow the injector before changing production settings.
content: When to use: A scenario runner or test harness is intentionally driving the registration path beyond steady-state capacity.
Action: Reduce the request rate, lower the subscriber fan-out, and stagger start times so the platform can finish challenge-response cycles.
Why it works: Removing the burst source preserves realistic behavior and avoids hiding a generator problem behind infrastructure changes.
Verify: Incoming REGISTER rate tracks the new throttle settings and the queue drains without emergency scaling.
Follow-up: Record the safe traffic envelope so the same lab profile does not recreate the storm later.

articles.title: Validate the authentication challenge cache on the registration path
articles.summary: Challenge cache misses can make a normal spike look like a registration failure wave.
content: When to use: Many REGISTER requests oscillate between 401 and another retry without progressing to a stable 200 response.
Action: Inspect whether challenge state is being lost across replicas, confirm sticky behavior where required, and check that challenge TTL values match the expected transaction duration.
Why it works: Lost authentication state turns one registration into several incomplete attempts, multiplying load and confusing the RCA signal.
Verify: The ratio of repeated 401 responses per subscriber should fall and successful registrations should complete in fewer hops.
Rollback: Revert challenge-cache changes if they introduce stale authorization data or cross-subscriber leakage.

articles.title: Rebalance the SIP edge load balancer when one registration shard is hot
articles.summary: Uneven edge distribution can overload one shard even when overall capacity looks healthy.
content: When to use: A subset of P-CSCF pods is saturated while peers remain mostly idle and REGISTER failures cluster by ingress endpoint.
Action: Review the SIP load-balancing policy, endpoint health, and any stale target list so new traffic fans out evenly across healthy pods.
Why it works: Registration storms often begin as localized hot spots before the rest of the control plane shows distress.
Verify: Per-pod request distribution normalizes and the hottest pod no longer dominates latency or retransmission graphs.
Evidence to capture: Endpoint counts, pod-level request rates, and the active load-balancer policy revision.

articles.title: Review registration expiry values that are forcing premature renewals
articles.summary: Overly short expiry windows can create a self-inflicted registration storm.
content: When to use: Registrations succeed, but they return again too quickly and the platform cycles subscribers through the same path repeatedly.
Action: Compare the configured registration expiry, refresh margin, and UE expectations so the system is not requesting renewals too frequently.
Why it works: A bad expiry policy can generate sustained high REGISTER rates even when the network and application are otherwise healthy.
Verify: Subscriber refresh spacing lengthens and steady-state REGISTER rate returns to the normal envelope.
Guardrail: Apply expiry changes gradually because some clients react poorly to abrupt policy shifts.

articles.title: Isolate a broken subscriber batch that is poisoning the registration flow
articles.summary: A bad import or credential batch can create repeat registration failures that mimic a platform outage.
content: When to use: Registration rejects are concentrated around one subscriber segment, one test batch, or one recently imported profile set.
Action: Correlate failing public identities or IMSIs, quarantine the affected batch, and verify the rest of the subscriber population can still register cleanly.
Why it works: Removing the bad cohort quickly reduces noise and separates subscriber-data defects from core signaling failures.
Verify: The 4xx ratio falls for unaffected subscribers and new incidents stop clustering around the same identity family.
Escalate if: Multiple batches fail after the same policy or HSS change, which points to a broader control-plane issue.

articles.title: Drain and replace a stuck P-CSCF pod that is holding bad registration state
articles.summary: Long-lived stale state on one pod can keep rejecting or delaying requests after the root cause is gone.
content: When to use: Registration failures stay pinned to one instance even after traffic pressure falls or after upstream dependencies recover.
Action: Remove the suspect pod from service, let in-flight transactions drain if possible, and start a clean replacement with the same revision.
Why it works: A stateful defect in one worker can keep replaying the failure pattern while healthy peers behave normally.
Verify: The incident follows the pod out of rotation and new replicas show normal transaction completion.
Risk: Draining the wrong pod during peak load can shift traffic onto already stressed peers, so confirm residual capacity first.

articles.title: Verify the downstream S-CSCF capacity path before blaming the edge
articles.summary: Registration symptoms seen on the P-CSCF edge can be caused by downstream S-CSCF saturation.
content: When to use: Edge latency is high but the strongest pressure appears after the request is forwarded to the S-CSCF or service-logic tier.
Action: Compare P-CSCF ingress volume with S-CSCF processing latency, worker saturation, and queue depth before applying another edge-only fix.
Why it works: The first visible failure is not always the first overloaded hop, and scaling the wrong tier can prolong the incident.
Verify: End-to-end registration timing improves only when the downstream bottleneck is relieved.
Operator hint: Pair this check with topology or service metrics so the RCA can show where the delay actually formed.

articles.title: Capture the dominant REGISTER reject codes before changing policy
articles.summary: Registration failures should be grouped by the real reject code, not treated as one generic symptom.
content: When to use: The platform is returning mixed 401, 403, 408, or 5xx responses and operators are considering a broad policy rollback.
Action: Bucket failures by SIP status code, map them to their responsible service, and confirm which failure class actually dominates the incident window.
Why it works: Fixes for authentication rejects, transport timeouts, and capacity overloads are different even though they all present as registration failure.
Verify: The chosen remediation targets the dominant failure bucket and the incident narrative reflects that narrower diagnosis.
Documentation: Attach the top reject-code distribution to the incident so future knowledge articles can rank more precisely.
